metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

records.notes: Old Man of the Woods is kept under the common-name slug. The former scientific-name slug strobilomyces-strobilaceus has been merged here to avoid duplicate listings.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Usually skipped. If used by experienced foragers, only young firm pieces are worth considering.
records.edibility_detail: Not a prime edible. Use only young firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor textured. Treat as a novelty/caution bolete, not a beginner meal.
records.general_notes: Hard to confuse with choice boletes because of the shaggy black-gray cap and blackening flesh. Species-level Strobilomyces taxonomy can be fiddly.
records.field_identification: Distinctive gray to black shaggy/scaly bolete with woolly cap and stem. Pores start whitish gray and darken; cut flesh often reddens or pinks, then darkens toward black. Overall look is sooty and ragged.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Old Man of the Woods

texture: Firm when young; tough or spongy when old

records.edibility_notes: Edible by some accounts when young, but usually poor quality; included mainly because it is distinctive and useful for ID practice. Not a prime edible.

edible_use.notes: Not a prime edible. Use only young firm specimens if at all; older specimens are poor textured. Treat as a novelty/caution bolete, not a beginner meal.

records.duplicate_note: Duplicate Old Man of the Woods record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

records.mushroom_card_note: Sooty shaggy bolete; interesting ID, mediocre food.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible oyster mushroom when fresh and confidently identified. Cook before eating; discard old, waterlogged, yellowing, or strongly fishy specimens.
records.general_notes: A core gilled mushroom entry for the guide. Keep separate from the warmer-season phoenix oyster and the aspen-focused oyster entry.

mushroom_profile.season_note: October through early April in cool weather on hardwoods; separate from warmer-season oyster species.

records.edibility_detail: Confirm wood growth, decurrent pale gills, no ring or volva, and a white to pale lilac spore print. Do not treat every pale shelf mushroom as an oyster.
records.field_identification: Overlapping shelf-like clusters on hardwood, with white to grayish caps, whitish decurrent gills running down a short lateral stem or pseudostem, firm white flesh, and a white to faintly lilac spore print.
records.mushroom_card_note: Classic cool-weather hardwood oyster; confirm decurrent gills and no ring/volva.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.general_notes: Use this entry for the thinner, warmer-season oyster type rather than treating every oyster as a fall pearl oyster.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late April through September; warm-weather oyster mushroom that can begin in late spring.

records.mushroom_card_note: Warm-season oyster type; confirm wood growth, decurrent gills, and lack of ring/volva.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after confident oyster-mushroom identification and normal cooking. Confirm hardwood growth, decurrent pale gills, white flesh, no ring or volva, and warm-season timing; do not use if the specimen is old, buggy, strongly off-smelling, or growing in a way that suggests a different gilled mushroom.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Pleurotus pulmonarius
use_links.notes: Warm-season oyster traits, hardwood substrate, decurrent gills, spore print, and separation from P. ostreatus and P. populinus.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook like other chanterelles; best used fresh because small fruitbodies dry out quickly.
records.general_notes: Tiny red chanterelles are easy to overlook. The false-gill ridges and soil growth are more important than color alone.

records.field_identification: Small red to cinnabar-orange chanterelle with blunt forked ridges running down the stem, not thin blade-like true gills. Grows on forest soil, often in mossy hardwood or mixed woods.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible after confirming blunt false gills/ridges and chanterelle structure. Avoid orange gilled mushrooms on wood, especially jack-o-lanterns.
records.mushroom_card_note: Small cinnabar chanterelle: blunt decurrent ridges, not sharp gills.

use_links.label: MSU: Cantharellus cinnabarinus
use_links.notes: Michigan/Great Lakes context for cinnabar chanterelles.

stem_feature: Same color or slightly paler stem

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confirming blunt false gills/ridges and chanterelle structure. Avoid orange gilled mushrooms on wood, especially jack-o-lanterns.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Not edible as food. Keep as an ID/practical-use bracket fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Not edible as food; hard, perennial, woody bracket. Keep as an identification/practical-use record only. North American red-belted-conk material may represent Fomitopsis mounceae/schrenkii-style members of the former F. pinicola complex rather than strict Eurasian F. pinicola, so this app treats the name as a cautious group label.
records.general_notes: Common northern conk. Compare with artist’s conk and hemlock varnish shelf; red-belted conk lacks the glossy varnished reishi surface and has a distinct banded hoof/shelf form.

edible_use.notes: Not a food mushroom; tough woody conk.

images_structured.title: Red-belted conk field view
images_structured.author: George Chernilevsky
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata copied from local credit table (credits-enrichment-v109); source page retained for attribution.

records.field_identification: Hard woody perennial bracket with a pale growing margin, darker older zones, and often a reddish-orange band near the edge. Underside is pored and pale; grows on dead conifer or hardwood logs, stumps, and snags.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hard banded conk with red belt; useful ID/practical species, not food.
records.other_uses: Can be used as a teaching/display bracket. Some traditional tinder/practical interest exists for bracket fungi, but this app treats it mainly as a woody non-food ID entry.
records.taxonomy_notes: North American red-belted conks have been split from strict Eurasian Fomitopsis pinicola in modern treatments; use this app record as a practical field group, not a final microscope/DNA-level determination.

records.culinary_uses: Emergency/survival-use lichen only: historical use involved soaking/boiling or alkaline processing. Not recommended as a routine edible.
records.edibility_detail: Not a normal wild food in this guide. Historically used as famine/survival food only after extensive processing to reduce bitterness and acids; avoid casual eating.

mushroom_profile.summary: A lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically processed as famine food or flour extender, but bitter acids require substantial preparation.
host_trees: None; may occur in boreal/open pine woods but does not fruit from wood
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round; slow-growing and persistent rather than a short mushroom flush.
research_notes: Better treated as a lichen entry than a mushroom entry.
Known more as animal browse and famine food than as a casual wild edible.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
fertile_surface: Lichen thallus; no mushroom gills or pores

records.field_identification: Pale gray-white, highly branched fruticose lichen forming dry cushiony mats on the ground, not a moss and not a gilled mushroom. Branches repeatedly divide into small forked tips; look for open, well-drained boreal pine woods, barrens, heaths, sandy soil, humus, or soil over rock. Slow growth and lichen ecology are key ID/use clues; harvest pressure should be low.

field_measurements.overall: Mats/cushions up to about 10 cm tall / up to about 4 in tall
field_measurements.thickness: Thicker branches usually about 1–1.5 mm wide / about 1/32–1/16 in wide
field_measurements.notes: Moister sites may produce taller podetia to roughly 12 cm / about 4.7 in; drier sites often shorter, about 4–6 cm / 1.6–2.4 in.

records.other_uses: Commonly noted as reindeer/caribou forage and sometimes used decoratively; slow growth makes heavy harvesting a bad idea.
completion_gaps: Record is a lichen-group entry, not a single casual food mushroom; edibility remains emergency-only.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional source of vitamin C in survival situations
records.culinary_uses: Emergency food only when properly processed; not a normal table mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Survival/emergency-food history only; not recommended as a routine edible. Processing/boiling is needed in traditional accounts and heavy harvesting of slow-growing lichens is poor practice.
records.scientific_name: Umbilicaria spp.; representative detail from Umbilicaria mammulata

mushroom_profile.summary: Actually a rock-dwelling lichen rather than a mushroom. Historically used as an emergency or famine food after extensive processing.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round where established; not a short fruiting-season mushroom.
research_notes: Common name covers edible Umbilicaria lichens rather than a single mushroom species.
Treat as a survival food entry, not a routine edible.
Slow-growing and best not heavily harvested.
Source coverage count in current app data: 2
fertile_surface: Lichen thallus; no mushroom gills or pores

records.field_identification: Large, leathery foliose lichen attached to rock by a central holdfast/navel-like point, usually forming dark, rounded, leaf-like thalli on boulders or steep rock walls. Upper surface is reddish-brown to gray-brown; underside can be dark to black. ID depends on rock substrate, central attachment, leathery thallus, and lichen—not mushroom—structure.

field_measurements.overall: Representative U. mammulata thallus usually about 4–15 cm across / about 1.6–5.9 in across
field_measurements.notes: Exceptional U. mammulata specimens reported to 63 cm / about 2.1 ft across. This app entry covers rock-tripe lichens broadly, so the range is representative, not every Umbilicaria species.

records.other_uses: Useful as an ID/lichen ecology entry. Slow-growing rock lichens should be observed more than harvested.
completion_gaps: Record is a broad rock-tripe lichen group; measurements use U. mammulata as representative rather than every Umbilicaria species.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Only for experienced foragers who can narrow the species/group and cook thoroughly; avoid old, soft, bug-ridden, or uncertain collections.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Leccinum scabrum to illustrate the broader scaber-stalk birch/aspen bolete group.
records.duplicate_note: Broad scaber-stalk duplicate/group page kept only as a legacy slug alias. Its caution content has been consolidated into Birch Bolete / Leccinum scabrum.
records.scientific_name: Leccinum scabrum group / Leccinum spp. with scabers
records.field_identification: Bolete group with pores instead of gills and a stem covered in dark scabers/dots or rough flecks. Birch/aspen association is a major ID clue. Representative birch bolete features: tan to brown cap, whitish pores aging gray, slim tapering stem with blackish scabers, and mycorrhizal association with birch. Treat orange-capped/aspen scaber-stalks separately because some Leccinum-type boletes have caused illness when undercooked or misidentified.

field_measurements.cap: Representative L. scabrum cap about 4–10 cm wide / about 1.5–4 in wide
field_measurements.stem: Representative L. scabrum stem about 5–15 cm long and 1–3.5 cm wide / about 2–6 in long and 3/8–1 3/8 in wide
field_measurements.notes: Group record: representative L. scabrum measurements, not a definitive range for every scaber-stalk bolete.

host_trees: Aspen / poplar for some related scaber-stalk boletes
pore_color: Whitish when young; grayish with age in representative birch bolete
stem_feature: Scabers / dark rough dots on stem
cap_surface: Tan to brown cap in representative birch bolete
mushroom_profile.season_note: Representative birch bolete fruits in summer to fall; North American timing varies by region.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Brown to olive-brown in many field guides for Leccinum-type boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Group-level caution: some scaber-stalk boletes are eaten when firm and thoroughly cooked, but species-level ID matters and orange/aspen Leccinum-type boletes have illness reports. Keep this as a caution group, not a blanket edible.

Keep as needs_review until this broad group is either split into species records or explicitly retained as a caution-group page.

records.notes: Fast-deliquescing gilled mushroom of disturbed ground.
records.culinary_uses: Cook promptly the same day; does not store well because it deliquesces into black ink.
records.general_notes: Good beginner ID shape, but timing is everything. Alcohol warnings belong more strongly to some inky cap relatives, but old/inky shaggy manes are still not worth eating.

ring: Ring may be present or movable

records.field_identification: Tall white shaggy cylindrical cap with upturned scales, blackening and dissolving from the lower edge as it matures. Gills start white, then pink, then black inky; usually in lawns, roadsides, trails, and disturbed ground.
records.mushroom_card_note: White shaggy cylinder; eat only young white-gilled specimens, before the ink show starts.

records.edibility_detail: Edible only when very young with white gills and firm white flesh. Once pink, black, slimy, or inky, skip it.

edible_use.notes: Edible only when very young with white gills and firm white flesh. Once pink, black, slimy, or inky, skip it.

records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Usually not a top table mushroom on its own; better known as a host for lobster mushroom. Use only firm, mild-tasting specimens after confident ID.
records.general_notes: A good reminder that russulas belong in the gilled lane too. The short thick stem and brittle white flesh matter more than generic 'mushroom' features.
records.field_identification: Large, dense white to cream russula with a very short stem, brittle flesh, thick crowded gills that may run slightly down the stem, and no ring or volva. Often partly buried or dirty.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conservative white-russula entry; useful mainly as a lobster-mushroom host.

records.edibility_detail: Edible by some foragers, but usually firm, bland, and often parasitized into lobster mushrooms. Keep ID conservative and avoid acrid or unknown white russulas.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Russula brevipes
use_links.notes: Short-stemmed white russula traits and comparison notes.

records.display_name: Shrimp of the woods / aborted entoloma
records.common_name: Shrimp of the woods / aborted entoloma
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently show the Hypomyces lactifluorum / lobster-mushroom side of the concept pending a stronger aborted-entoloma image set.

images_structured.title: Shrimp of the woods / aborted entoloma

records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Use only mild-tasting, confidently identified shrimp russulas. Cook and sample conservatively; the fishy/shrimp odor and staining pattern are key clues, not permission to guess.
records.scientific_name: Russula xerampelina group / North American shrimp russulas
records.field_identification: Russula with brittle flesh, no ring or volva, wine-red to purple-brown cap tones, mild taste, and a distinctive shrimp/fishy odor that develops especially with age or handling; stem and gills may discolor yellowish to brownish.
records.mushroom_card_note: Edible russula group, but do not wing it: confirm mild taste, brittle russula structure, and shrimp/fishy odor.

records.edibility_detail: Shrimp russulas are edible when the ID is solid, but russulas are a difficult group. Avoid acrid, unknown, or poorly identified russulas.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Russula xerampelina
use_links.notes: Shrimp-russula traits, odor, staining, and group caution.

records.culinary_uses: Cook fresh like other chanterelles; good sautéed, in cream sauces, eggs, or dried if clean.
records.general_notes: Smooth chanterelles can fool people expecting obvious ridges. The underside may be nearly smooth, but it should not have thin blade-like gills.
records.field_identification: Orange-yellow chanterelle with a mostly smooth to shallowly wrinkled underside rather than deep blade-like gills. Grows on soil in hardwood woods; flesh is solid, with fruity/apricot odor when fresh.
records.mushroom_card_note: Chanterelle with a smooth/wrinkled underside; soil-grown, fruity, solid flesh.

underside: False gills / ridges weak or absent
stem_feature: Same color to paler than cap

records.edibility_detail: Choice edible after confirming chanterelle structure, soil growth, solid flesh, and lack of true sharp gills. Avoid orange gilled clusters on wood.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible after confirming chanterelle structure, soil growth, solid flesh, and lack of true sharp gills. Avoid orange gilled clusters on wood.

records.notes: Hard hoof-shaped bracket on hardwoods, especially birch and beech. The tough gray-brown hoof form and pale pore underside are the key field marks.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: Not food; included for tinder/utility value and for separating woody conks from edible shelf fungi.

images_structured.title: Tinder conk / hoof fungus on wood

records.photo_review_cleanup_note: Removed from Needs Review because the only explicit review reasons were image/photo-related and a real Commons-backed image is now available.

records.medicinal_uses: Traditional and supplement interest, especially as tea/extract; this app does not treat it as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Too tough for table eating; usually dried and simmered for tea/decoction.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Thin leathery shelf with strongly zoned, fuzzy cap surface and a true tiny-pored white underside.
records.mushroom_card_note: Tea/decoction mushroom, not table food; confirm true pores underneath.
